metadata.title: Privacy Policy
metadata.description: Privacy Policy for Open Model Lab covering browser storage, optional accounts, account-linked progress sync, billing, feedback, analytics, and ads.

hero.title: Privacy Policy
hero.description: Plain-English details about what Open Model Lab stores, syncs, and sends in the current product: local-first progress, optional accounts with account-linked sync for signed-in learners, Stripe-hosted billing, and separate analytics or ad switches when enabled.

browser.title: Browser storage comes first
browser.paragraph1: {siteName} saves learning progress in this browser by default so the core product works signed out.
browser.paragraph2: That local progress can include concept history, continue-learning state, review cues, and other study state tied to this browser. Clearing site data or switching browsers can remove or separate that local state.

accounts.title: Accounts and synced study data stay bounded
accounts.paragraph1: If you sign in, the app stores the account information needed to authenticate the session and resolve free versus Supporter access.
accounts.paragraph2: Core learning progress sync uses the existing account backend for any signed-in account, while Supporter-gated features such as saved setups, saved compare setups, exact-state sharing, and advanced study tools stay bounded to the Supporter layer where those features are actually implemented.

feedback.title: Feedback and support messages are optional
feedback.paragraph1: If you use the feedback form, the app can send your message, page context, and any reply contact you include to the configured support inbox through the existing server-side delivery path.
feedback.paragraph2: If direct delivery is unavailable, the site keeps the mailto fallback visible instead. Do not send sensitive information you would not want copied into a support thread.

billing.title: Stripe handles checkout and billing
billing.paragraph1: Supporter checkout and subscription management are handled through Stripe-hosted pages rather than custom card forms inside the app.
billing.paragraph2: The product stores only the billing facts needed to connect a Stripe customer or subscription to your account and update the app's Supporter entitlement state.

ads.title: Ads and analytics are optional and separate
ads.paragraph1: This deployment may enable lightweight analytics for page and learning-signal events. Analytics is optional and separate from advertising.
ads.paragraph2: When ads are enabled, the product uses manually placed Google AdSense units on selected discovery pages only, not on the main concept lab or challenge surfaces.

choices.title: You keep basic choices
choices.paragraph1: You can use the core learning product signed out, sign in only when you want account features, or contact support with questions about privacy, billing, or saved data.
choices.paragraph2: For billing-specific questions, use the public billing path below or email support directly.

adsBlock.label: Advertising and third parties
adsBlock.paragraph1: When advertising is enabled, {siteName} uses manually placed Google AdSense units on selected discovery pages. Supporter accounts stay ad-free.
adsBlock.paragraph2: Those advertising requests may involve Google receiving standard browser, device, and page information under Google's own systems and policies. The app keeps advertising off the interactive concept lab, challenge flow, and other core teaching surfaces.
adsBlock.paragraph3Middle: for the current placement policy, or use
adsBlock.paragraph3Suffix: if you need to ask about this launch version directly.

support.label: Questions
support.descriptionSuffix: . Billing questions can use the same inbox or the billing page.

metadata.title: Terms
metadata.description: Plain-English terms for Open Model Lab's public site, interactive learning content, optional accounts, Supporter access, and support paths.

hero.title: Plain-English terms for using the site, its accounts, and its optional Supporter layer.
hero.description: These terms are intentionally readable and tied to the current product instead of pretending the site has a larger legal or operational system than it really does.

responsible.title: Use the site responsibly
responsible.paragraph1: {siteName} is an educational website. You may browse the public catalog, use the simulations, create or use an account, and contact support in ordinary ways.
responsible.paragraph2: Do not use the service to interfere with the site, scrape it aggressively, bypass product limits, abuse support paths, or try to access accounts or data that are not yours.

educational.title: The service is educational, not professional advice
educational.paragraph1: The content is meant to help people learn science concepts more clearly through explanations, worked examples, and simulations.
educational.paragraph2: Do not treat the service as professional engineering, safety, medical, legal, financial, or other high-stakes advice.

accounts.title: Accounts and Supporter stay bounded
accounts.paragraph1: Signing in is optional. Signing in alone does not upgrade the account.
accounts.paragraph2: The current product offers one {planName} subscription through Stripe, while the app itself still uses the existing bounded free-versus-Supporter entitlement model for access checks.

misuse.title: Do not misuse the service
misuse.paragraph1: Do not upload, submit, or send anything illegal, abusive, malicious, or deliberately misleading through the site or its support paths.
misuse.paragraph2: Do not use the product in ways meant to break the simulations, degrade availability, probe for security weaknesses, or create fake billing or support activity.

ownership.title: Site materials remain owned by the project
ownership.paragraph1: Open Model Lab and its published materials, including the site copy, simulations, visual assets, and authored learning content, remain owned by the project or its licensors.
ownership.paragraph2: You keep ownership of the feedback or messages you send, but you permit the project to review and use them to operate, improve, and support the service.

access.title: Access can change or end
access.paragraph1: The service may change, pause, or remove features over time, including Supporter features, content coverage, and third-party integrations.
access.paragraph2: Accounts or access may be limited, suspended, or ended when needed to protect the service, other users, or the project.

availability.title: Availability is not guaranteed
availability.paragraph1: The service is provided on an as-available basis. Bugs, downtime, third-party outages, and billing or deployment issues can happen.
availability.paragraph2: The project does not promise uninterrupted availability, error-free content, or fitness for every use case.

home.title: Home
home.description: The home page gives a quick route into live concept labs, starter tracks, guided paths, search, and recent progress.

hints.one: Use Start if you want the site to suggest a first step.
hints.two: Open Concepts when you already know the subject or topic you want.
hints.three: The resume area appears when this browser has local or synced progress.

start.title: Start
start.description: Start helps you pick a bounded next lesson from your interests, confidence, time, and existing progress.

hints.one: Change the interest, confidence, and time choices to reshape the recommendations.
hints.two: Primary recommendations point to real concepts, tracks, topics, or subject pages.
hints.three: Progress in this browser can change the continue-learning suggestions.

search.title: Search and discovery
search.description: Search spans concepts, subjects, topics, starter tracks, guided paths, and goal paths from the canonical catalog.

hints.one: Use the search box for a term, concept, subject, or track name.
hints.two: Subject and topic filters narrow browsing without creating a separate catalog.
hints.three: Result groups keep subjects, topics, tracks, guided paths, and concepts distinct.

conceptLibrary.title: Concepts
conceptLibrary.description: The concept library is the broad catalog entry point for subjects, topics, starter tracks, and individual live concept pages.

hints.one: Subject and topic routes come from the same catalog as search and start.
hints.two: Concept cards open live labs with guided explanation, practice, and progress cues.
hints.three: Use Start if you want help choosing rather than browsing.

subject.title: Subject page
subject.description: Subject pages collect related topics, starter tracks, and first concepts without changing concept identity.

hints.one: Featured topics show the main branches in this subject.
hints.two: Starter tracks provide a sequenced path when you want structure.
hints.three: Progress cues reuse the same local-first progress snapshot as the rest of the site.

topic.title: Topic page
topic.description: Topic pages narrow the catalog to one branch and surface the clearest concepts and practice for that branch.

hints.one: Best-first concepts are good entry points when the topic is new.
hints.two: Practice links open the shared assessment runner for this topic.
hints.three: Related concepts remain part of the same canonical catalog.

concept.title: Live concept page
concept.description: Concept pages center the live lab first, then add guided steps, worked examples, quick tests, sharing, and progress support.

hints.one: Start with the live lab: change one control and read the scene or graph.
hints.two: Use the guided phase rail when you want a step-by-step lesson.
hints.three: Stable section links are available to everyone; exact-state sharing is a Supporter capability.

guided.title: Guided paths
guided.description: Guided paths collect concepts, tracks, challenges, and route steps into a sequenced learning flow.

hints.one: Open a collection when you want a curated order instead of free browsing.
hints.two: Concept bundles and assignments reuse the existing concept and track routes.
hints.three: Signed-in accounts can save some guided-study objects across devices.

guidedDetail.title: Guided collection
guidedDetail.description: A guided collection shows the ordered steps and links you through the existing learning surfaces.

hints.one: Steps can point to concepts, tracks, challenges, or route-based study surfaces.
hints.two: Bundle and assignment tools keep the collection tied to real concept links.
hints.three: Progress cues come from the shared learner-state model.

challenges.title: Challenges
challenges.description: Challenges highlight concept tasks that ask you to apply a model rather than only inspect it.

hints.one: Challenge cards link back to the concept runtime that owns the interaction.
hints.two: Completions can feed achievements and dashboard progress.
hints.three: Use filters and recommendations to find a challenge that fits your current subject.

tests.title: Practice
tests.description: The test hub collects concept, topic, and pack practice in one place.

hints.one: Search and subject filters help narrow the available assessments.
hints.two: Resume cues come from browser-local assessment sessions.
hints.three: Completion and review state are separate from exact same-browser resume state.

assessment.title: Assessment
assessment.description: Assessment pages let you practice concept, topic, and pack questions with saved same-browser progress.

hints.one: In-progress resume is local to this browser.
hints.two: Completed results can update the same progress model used by recommendations.
hints.three: Use the test hub to switch to a different assessment scope.

tools.title: Learning tools
tools.description: The tools hub lists specialized workspaces that sit beside the main concept pages.

hints.one: Open Circuit Builder for a dedicated circuit workspace.
hints.two: Open the chemistry reaction mind map for functional-group routes.
hints.three: Tool state stays inside each tool's own bounded model.

chemistryTool.title: Chemistry reaction mind map
chemistryTool.description: The chemistry map lets you inspect functional-group families and reaction pathways visually.

hints.one: Click a family node for group properties.
hints.two: Click a pathway edge for reagents, conditions, equations, and notes.
hints.three: Use the route explorer to trace short directed paths through the graph.

circuitBuilder.title: Circuit Builder
circuitBuilder.description: Circuit Builder is a standalone workspace for building, inspecting, saving, and exporting simple circuits.

hints.one: Add parts from the component library, then connect terminals with the wire tool.
hints.two: The inspector shows editable values and solved circuit feedback for the current selection.
hints.three: Local drafts and saved circuits stay separate from account-backed Supporter saves.

track.title: Starter track
track.description: Starter tracks sequence concepts into a bounded path with completion and recap surfaces.

hints.one: Follow the next recommended step when you want a guided order.
hints.two: Track progress is derived from the same concept progress snapshot.
hints.three: Completion pages summarize the path without creating a separate learning model.

assignment.title: Assignment
assignment.description: Assignments package guided concept links into a shareable study surface for signed-in users.

hints.one: Assignment steps still resolve to existing concept, track, or guided routes.
hints.two: Progress cues use the same account and local-progress seams.
hints.three: Use the dashboard to return to assigned guided work.

dashboard.title: Dashboard
dashboard.description: The dashboard brings together progress, achievements, assignments, saved study tools, and account state.

hints.one: Signed-in free accounts can sync canonical progress across devices.
hints.two: Supporter adds saved study tools, richer analytics, and ad-free browsing.
hints.three: Achievement and reward state is account-scoped.

analytics.title: Learning analytics
analytics.description: Analytics summarizes progress, review needs, strengths, and usage from the same learner-state inputs.

hints.one: Analytics is richer for Supporter accounts but does not replace progress.
hints.two: Review cues come from concept and assessment activity.
hints.three: Checkpoint history uses bounded synced progress history.

account.title: Account and sync
account.description: The account surface handles optional sign-in, progress sync, Supporter state, and saved study tools.

hints.one: Open Model Lab remains usable while signed out.
hints.two: Signed-in free accounts can sync progress.
hints.three: Supporter controls are based on the resolved entitlement capabilities.

trust.title: Site information
trust.description: Public trust and support pages explain billing, privacy, terms, ads, contact, or product background.

hints.one: These pages describe what the product actually supports now.
hints.two: Feedback and support links stay available through the shared site chrome.
hints.three: Account, billing, and ads behavior follows the existing entitlement and policy seams.

default.title: This page
default.description: This page is part of the Open Model Lab learning surface and uses the shared navigation and progress model.

hints.one: Use the header to move between Start, Concepts, Guided paths, Challenges, Practice, and Tools.
hints.two: Help stays available even if you turn off the first-time prompt.
hints.three: Most learning state is local-first, with optional account sync when signed in.

pageOverview.title: Start with the current page
pageOverview.body: The main content area is the working surface. Open Model Lab keeps routes focused: browse, practice, run a live lab, or inspect account progress without a mandatory tutorial.

homeActions.title: Choose a starting route
homeActions.body: Start gives you a recommendation. Concepts opens the full catalog. The surrounding links go to search, subjects, guided paths, and challenges.

startChooser.title: Tune the recommendation
startChooser.body: These choices reshape the suggested next step without saving a separate profile. You can change them at any time.

startRecommendations.title: Open the suggested step
startRecommendations.body: Recommendations point to real concepts, tracks, topics, or subject pages, so the tour never sends you to a fake onboarding route.

searchInput.title: Search the catalog
searchInput.body: Search covers concepts, subjects, topics, starter tracks, guided paths, and goal paths from the same discovery graph.

searchFilters.title: Narrow by subject or topic
searchFilters.body: Filters refine the current catalog view. Clearing them returns you to the broader discovery surface.

searchResults.title: Read the result groups
searchResults.body: Result counts and groups keep different item types clear, so a topic page, concept, track, or guided path does not look interchangeable.

conceptLiveLab.title: Use the live lab first
conceptLiveLab.body: Concept pages are lab-first. Change one control, watch the scene, and read the graph or status before moving into examples or tests.

conceptPhaseRail.title: Follow the guided lesson rail
conceptPhaseRail.body: The phase rail turns the same live lab into a shorter lesson flow. It helps when you want prompts instead of open exploration.

testHubControls.title: Find the right practice
testHubControls.body: Search and subject filters narrow concept, topic, and pack assessments without burying the next useful practice.

testHubResults.title: Use resume and progress cues
testHubResults.body: The test hub shows resume and completion cues from assessment sessions and progress, then links into the matching assessment page.

toolsDirectory.title: Open a specialized workspace
toolsDirectory.body: Tools are focused workspaces that sit beside concept pages, such as Circuit Builder and the chemistry reaction mind map.

chemistryGraph.title: Inspect the reaction map
chemistryGraph.body: The main map keeps the functional-group families and pathways visible at once, so you can start by reading the chemistry landscape before drilling into one node or edge.

chemistryRouteExplorer.title: Trace routes between families
chemistryRouteExplorer.body: Use the route explorer controls to choose a start and target group, then surface short bounded pathways through the reaction graph.

chemistryInspector.title: Use the side inspector
chemistryInspector.body: The right-hand inspector explains the current node, pathway, comparison, or route results so the map and the details stay linked.

circuitWorkspace.title: Build in the workspace
circuitWorkspace.body: The center canvas is where parts, wires, and circuit state live. It has its own document model and export behavior.

circuitPalette.title: Add circuit parts
circuitPalette.body: The component library is where you add parts or search for them. It stays separate from saved circuits and account saves.

circuitInspector.title: Inspect the selection
circuitInspector.body: The inspector explains the selected part or wire and exposes editable values when the current selection supports them.

mainNavigation.title: Move across the main surfaces
mainNavigation.body: The header links use the product's real route names: Start, Concepts, Guided paths, Challenges, Practice, and Tools.

continueLearning.title: Resume or start learning
continueLearning.body: This action points to Start. When this browser has progress, the label changes to Continue learning.

accountSync.title: Account and sync are optional
accountSync.body: Signed-out use is supported. Signing in lets free accounts sync progress, while Supporter adds saved study tools, analytics, sharing, and ad-free browsing.

helpEntry.title: Reopen help any time
helpEntry.body: The Help button stays available even if you skip or disable the first-time prompt.

ShareLinksPanel.description: Copy a stable entry point. Links stay public and never include local progress or live control state.

metadata.title: About
metadata.description: The founder story behind Open Model Lab, plus concrete ways to support the simulation-first learning product.

hero.title: Learning should feel like exploring a system, not memorizing disconnected pieces.
hero.description: Open Model Lab grew out of a simple frustration: too much science learning rewards getting the answer without helping students build a clear picture of what is actually happening.

origin.title: Where it started
origin.p1: Open Model Lab started from a frustration I couldn't ignore.
origin.p2: When I was studying science in high school, I realized that a lot of the time, I didn't truly understand what I was learning. I could solve problems, get the right answers, and pass exams, but something felt off.
origin.p3: In physics, I was plugging numbers into equations without really understanding what those equations meant. In chemistry, I memorized reactions without seeing why they happened. In biology, I could recall facts, but I didn't have a clear mental picture of what was actually going on.
origin.p4: It felt like I was learning how to answer, not how to understand.
origin.p5: And I wasn't the only one.

mission.title: What the project is
mission.p1: That's why I started Open Model Lab.
mission.p2: The idea is simple: learning science should feel like exploring a system, not memorizing disconnected pieces. You should be able to see how things change, interact with them, and build intuition step by step.
mission.p3: Instead of just reading formulas, you can play with them.
mission.p4: Instead of memorizing results, you can discover them.

horizon.title: Where it is going
horizon.p1: Open Model Lab started with physics, but it is already growing into a broader simulation-first learning product across multiple subjects.
horizon.p2: Especially now, with artificial intelligence making factual knowledge easy to access, memorization alone matters less. What matters is understanding: being able to think clearly, connect ideas, and actually know what's going on.
horizon.p3: That's what this project is trying to build.

principles.label: What this project is trying to build

items.p1: Learning science should feel like exploring a system.
items.p2: You should be able to see how things change and build intuition step by step.
items.p3: What matters is understanding: being able to think clearly, connect ideas, and actually know what's going on.

rightNow.label: Right now
rightNow.body: Physics is still the broadest subject, but the live product now also includes math, chemistry, and a bounded computer-science pilot without losing the simulation-first learning feel.

whyNow.label: Why now
whyNow.body: As AI makes factual knowledge easier to retrieve, the lasting skill is understanding: connecting ideas, seeing cause and effect, and reasoning with confidence.

support.label: Support the project
support.title: Keep core interactive learning free while helping the project improve.

paragraphs.p1: Open Model Lab is meant to keep core interactive learning accessible. Concept pages, simulations, tools, guided paths, challenges, and basic practice are not paywalled.
paragraphs.p2: Supporter subscriptions at {priceLabel} help pay for hosting, maintenance, development, and future improvements while adding convenience features for people who want them.
paragraphs.p3: Donations and feedback are still useful ways to support the project. The paid tier is not a wall around basic learning.
